Reproduction of Dunvald et al. (2022): Statistical Analysis of a Clinical Pharmacokinetic DDI Study

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

Oral Midazolam (2 mg) with and without CYP3A4 Inducer — 12 Healthy Volunteers

Author

Pramod BR

Published

06 June 2026

Note

Reproducibility statement: This report reproduces the complete statistical analysis from Dunvald et al. (2022), Clinical and Translational Science, 15:1856–1866, DOI: 10.1111/cts.13305. All code is available at github.com/pbr26/midazolam-pk-dunvald2022.

1 Background

Pharmacokinetic drug–drug interaction (DDI) studies are essential for understanding how co-administered drugs affect the exposure of a probe substrate. Midazolam is the gold standard CYP3A4 probe substrate, recommended by both EMA and FDA for clinical DDI studies. Its plasma concentration reliably reflects intestinal and hepatic CYP3A activity.

This report reproduces the complete statistical analysis from Dunvald et al. (2022), a tutorial paper providing a mock DDI dataset and full R code for clinical PK analysis. The study simulates what would be observed when a CYP3A inducer increases midazolam clearance.

Primary endpoint: AUClast of midazolam with vs. without inducer, assessed via geometric mean ratio (GMR) and 95% confidence interval.

2 Methods

2.1 Study Design

A simulated two-period crossover study. Twelve healthy subjects received a single oral dose of 2 mg midazolam with and without a CYP3A4 inducer, separated by a washout period. Plasma samples were collected at 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, and 12 hours post-dose.

The mock dataset was generated using Berkeley Madonna v10.2.8 (one-compartment extravascular model, Runge-Kutta 4th order). Baseline t½ ranged 1.8–3.1 h; induction simulated by increasing ke by 19–72% per subject.

2.2 Statistical Analysis

PK parameters derived by noncompartmental analysis (NCA) using PKNCA with the linear-up/log-down trapezoidal method. Results presented as median (IQR). GMR with 95% CI compared exposure between arms using a paired t-test on log-transformed values. Bioequivalence no-effect boundaries: 0.80–1.25. Tmax compared with Wilcoxon signed-rank test.

3 Results

3.1 Participants

Code 
fmt_med_iqr <- function(x) {
  paste0(round(median(x),1),
         " (", round(quantile(x,.25),1),
         "–",  round(quantile(x,.75),1), ")")
}
fmt_n_pct <- function(x, lv) {
  n <- sum(x==lv, na.rm=TRUE)
  paste0(n," (",round(n/length(x)*100,0),"%)")
}

t1 <- tibble(
  Characteristic = c(
    "Number of subjects, n",
    "Age, years — median (IQR)",
    "Sex — Male, n (%)",
    "Sex — Female, n (%)",
    "Body weight, kg — median (IQR)",
    "BMI, kg/m² — median (IQR)",
    "eGFR, mL/min/1.73m² — median (IQR)"
  ),
  `All subjects (N=12)` = c(
    "12",
    fmt_med_iqr(demo$Age_years),
    fmt_n_pct(demo$Sex,"Male"),
    fmt_n_pct(demo$Sex,"Female"),
    fmt_med_iqr(demo$Weight_kg),
    fmt_med_iqr(demo$BMI),
    fmt_med_iqr(demo$eGFR_ml_min)
  )
)

kable(t1, align=c("l","c")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width=FALSE, position="left") %>%
  row_spec(0, bold=TRUE) %>%
  footnote(general="Self-controlled crossover design. Continuous: median (IQR). eGFR: estimated glomerular filtration rate.",
           general_title="Note: ", footnote_as_chunk=TRUE)

3.2 Plasma Concentration–Time Profiles

Code 
fig2_main <- ggplot(mean_conc,
    aes(x=TIME, y=mean_conc, colour=ARM, shape=ARM, group=ARM)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin=mean_conc-sd_conc, ymax=mean_conc+sd_conc),
                width=0.2, linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.7) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.85) +
  geom_point(size=2.8, fill="white", stroke=0.9) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values=c("No_inducer"=col_no_ind,"With_inducer"=col_with_ind),
    labels=c("No_inducer"="No inducer","With_inducer"="Inducer"),
    name=NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values=c("No_inducer"=24,"With_inducer"=21),
    labels=c("No_inducer"="No inducer","With_inducer"="Inducer"),
    name=NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks=c(0,2,4,6,8,10,12), limits=c(0,12)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,1.65), breaks=seq(0,1.5,by=0.5)) +
  labs(x="Time after dose (h)", y="Concentration (ng/mL)",
       title="Mean midazolam plasma concentration") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position=c(0.85,0.72),
        legend.background=element_rect(fill="white",colour="grey80",linewidth=0.3),
        legend.text=element_text(size=10),
        plot.title=element_text(size=12,face="bold"),
        axis.title=element_text(size=11),
        panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

fig2_inset <- ggplot(mean_conc %>% filter(TIME>0, mean_conc>0),
    aes(x=TIME, y=mean_conc, colour=ARM, group=ARM)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(shape=ARM), size=1.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels=label_log(digits=2), breaks=c(0.03,0.1,0.3,1.0)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=c("No_inducer"=col_no_ind,"With_inducer"=col_with_ind),guide="none") +
  scale_shape_manual(values=c("No_inducer"=24,"With_inducer"=21),guide="none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks=c(0,4,8,12), limits=c(0,12)) +
  labs(x="Time (h)", y="Conc (ng/mL)") +
  theme_bw(base_size=8) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        plot.background=element_rect(fill="white",colour="grey70",linewidth=0.3))

fig2_main + inset_element(fig2_inset, left=0.42, bottom=0.34, right=0.99, top=0.99)

Midazolam concentrations were consistently higher in the control arm across all timepoints. Both arms peaked at approximately t = 2 h (Tmax), indicating the inducer did not affect absorption but specifically accelerated elimination. The log-scale inset reveals a steeper descending slope in the inducer arm, directly visualising the accelerated elimination rate constant (λz).

3.3 Noncompartmental Analysis — Table 2

Code 
no_ind   <- nca_clean %>% filter(ARM=="No_inducer")   %>% arrange(ID)
with_ind <- nca_clean %>% filter(ARM=="With_inducer") %>% arrange(ID)

fmt <- function(x) {
  x <- na.omit(x)
  paste0(round(median(x),2),
         " (", round(quantile(x,.25),2),
         "–",  round(quantile(x,.75),2),")")
}

get_gmr <- function(param) gmr %>% filter(Parameter==param) %>% pull(GMR_CI)

t2 <- tibble(
  Parameter = c("AUC~last~, ng·h/mL","C~max~, ng/mL",
                "t~½~, h","CL/F, L/h","T~max~, h"),
  `Without inducer (median IQR)` = c(
    fmt(no_ind$AUClast), fmt(no_ind$Cmax),
    fmt(no_ind$t_half),  fmt(no_ind$CLF),
    paste0(median(no_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),
           " (",min(no_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),"–",max(no_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),")")
  ),
  `With inducer (median IQR)` = c(
    fmt(with_ind$AUClast), fmt(with_ind$Cmax),
    fmt(with_ind$t_half),  fmt(with_ind$CLF),
    paste0(median(with_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),
           " (",min(with_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),"–",max(with_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),")ᵃ")
  ),
  `GMR (95% CI)` = c(
    get_gmr("AUClast"), get_gmr("Cmax"),
    get_gmr("t_half"),  get_gmr("CL_F"), "NA"
  )
)

kable(t2, align=c("l","c","c","c"), escape=FALSE) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover","condensed"), full_width=TRUE) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold=TRUE) %>%
  row_spec(1, bold=TRUE) %>%
  footnote(
    alphabet="Wilcoxon signed-rank test, p < 0.05.",
    general=paste0("GMR = geometric mean ratio (with / without inducer). ",
                   "CI = 95% confidence interval (paired t-test, log-transformed). ",
                   "Bioequivalence no-effect boundaries: 0.80–1.25."),
    general_title="", footnote_as_chunk=TRUE)

3.4 Individual Pharmacokinetic Variability

Code 
clf_long <- nca_clean %>%
  select(ID, ARM, CLF) %>%
  mutate(ARM_label = ifelse(ARM=="No_inducer","No inducer","Inducer"),
         ARM_label = factor(ARM_label, levels=c("No inducer","Inducer")))

ggplot(clf_long, aes(x=ARM_label, y=CLF, group=ID)) +
  geom_line(colour="grey55", linewidth=0.65, alpha=0.75) +
  geom_point(aes(colour=ARM), size=3.2, alpha=0.9) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=c("No_inducer"=col_no_ind,"With_inducer"=col_with_ind),
                      guide="none") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(1500,6800), breaks=seq(2000,6000,1000),
                     labels=comma) +
  labs(x=NULL,
       y=expression("Apparent clearance CL/F (L h"^{-1}~")"),
       title="Individual midazolam CL/F",
       caption="Each line = one subject. Orange = no inducer. Green = with inducer.") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text=element_text(size=12),
        axis.title.y=element_text(size=11),
        plot.title=element_text(size=12, face="bold"),
        panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x=element_blank())

All 12 subjects showed increased CL/F with the inducer — confirming universal CYP3A4 induction with no non-responders. Inter-individual variability in the magnitude of induction reflects differences in baseline CYP3A4 expression and inducibility.

3.5 Primary Endpoint: AUClast GMR

Code 
auc <- gmr %>% filter(Parameter=="AUClast")
pct_red <- round((1 - auc$GMR) * 100, 0)

GMR for AUClast = 0.72 (95% CI: 0.72–0.73)

The inducer caused a 28% reduction in midazolam systemic exposure. The entire 95% CI lies below the 0.80 bioequivalence boundary — confirming a clinically significant DDI.

Supporting parameters:

  • Cmax GMR = 0.9 — peak concentration reduced, indicating gut-wall CYP3A contribution
  • t½ GMR = 0.78 — elimination half-life shortened, confirming accelerated hepatic metabolism
  • CL/F GMR = 1.36 — apparent clearance increased, reciprocal of AUC reduction

4 Discussion

The CYP3A4 inducer reduced midazolam AUClast by 28%, increased CL/F, and shortened t½. All findings confirm CYP3A4-mediated enzyme induction. The GMR of 0.72 with the entire 95% CI below 0.80 classifies this as a clinically significant DDI per EMA/FDA guidance.

This reproduction confirms that open-source R tools (PKNCA, ggplot2, tableone) can produce publication-quality clinical PK analyses matching published results.

4.1 Limitations

This analysis used a simulated dataset. Real studies additionally require CYP3A5 genotyping, 1-OH-midazolam metabolite measurement, and validated bioanalytical methods.

5 Conclusion

A CYP3A4 inducer significantly reduced midazolam systemic exposure (AUClast GMR = 0.72; 95% CI 0.72–0.73), confirming a clinically significant DDI with 28% reduction in exposure. Results reproduce Dunvald et al. (2022) Table 2 closely using PKNCA with linear-up/log-down trapezoidal NCA.

6 Reproducibility

title: "Reproduction of Dunvald et al. (2022): Statistical Analysis of a Clinical Pharmacokinetic DDI Study"
subtitle: "Oral Midazolam (2 mg) with and without CYP3A4 Inducer — 12 Healthy Volunteers"
author: "Pramod BR"
date: today
date-format: "DD MMMM YYYY"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true

---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
#| label: setup
#| code-fold: false

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(patchwork)
library(tableone)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(here)
library(pwr)

# Load all pre-computed data files
long_data  <- read_csv(here("data","processed","mock_data_long.csv"),        show_col_types=FALSE)
mean_conc  <- read_csv(here("data","processed","mean_concentrations.csv"),    show_col_types=FALSE)
demo       <- read_csv(here("data","processed","demographics.csv"),           show_col_types=FALSE)
nca_clean  <- read_csv(here("output","tables","nca_parameters_individual.csv"), show_col_types=FALSE)
gmr        <- read_csv(here("output","tables","gmr_results.csv"),             show_col_types=FALSE)

# Colour palette matching paper Figure 2
col_no_ind   <- "#E8832A"
col_with_ind <- "#2A9E4F"
```

---

::: {.callout-note}
**Reproducibility statement:** This report reproduces the complete statistical analysis from Dunvald et al. (2022), *Clinical and Translational Science*, 15:1856–1866, DOI: [10.1111/cts.13305](https://doi.org/10.1111/cts.13305). All code is available at [github.com/pbr26/midazolam-pk-dunvald2022](https://github.com/pbr26/midazolam-pk-dunvald2022).
:::

## Background

Pharmacokinetic drug–drug interaction (DDI) studies are essential for understanding how co-administered drugs affect the exposure of a probe substrate. **Midazolam** is the gold standard CYP3A4 probe substrate, recommended by both EMA and FDA for clinical DDI studies. Its plasma concentration reliably reflects intestinal and hepatic CYP3A activity.

This report reproduces the complete statistical analysis from Dunvald et al. (2022), a tutorial paper providing a mock DDI dataset and full R code for clinical PK analysis. The study simulates what would be observed when a CYP3A inducer increases midazolam clearance.

**Primary endpoint:** AUC~last~ of midazolam with vs. without inducer, assessed via geometric mean ratio (GMR) and 95% confidence interval.

---

## Methods

### Study Design

A simulated two-period crossover study. Twelve healthy subjects received a single oral dose of **2 mg midazolam** with and without a CYP3A4 inducer, separated by a washout period. Plasma samples were collected at 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, and 12 hours post-dose.

The mock dataset was generated using Berkeley Madonna v10.2.8 (one-compartment extravascular model, Runge-Kutta 4th order). Baseline t~½~ ranged 1.8–3.1 h; induction simulated by increasing k~e~ by 19–72% per subject.

### Statistical Analysis

PK parameters derived by noncompartmental analysis (NCA) using `PKNCA` with the linear-up/log-down trapezoidal method. Results presented as **median (IQR)**. GMR with 95% CI compared exposure between arms using a paired t-test on log-transformed values. Bioequivalence no-effect boundaries: 0.80–1.25. T~max~ compared with Wilcoxon signed-rank test.

---

## Results

### Participants

```{r table1}
#| label: tbl-demographics
#| tbl-cap: "Table 1. Demographic and clinical characteristics of 12 healthy subjects"

fmt_med_iqr <- function(x) {
  paste0(round(median(x),1),
         " (", round(quantile(x,.25),1),
         "–",  round(quantile(x,.75),1), ")")
}
fmt_n_pct <- function(x, lv) {
  n <- sum(x==lv, na.rm=TRUE)
  paste0(n," (",round(n/length(x)*100,0),"%)")
}

t1 <- tibble(
  Characteristic = c(
    "Number of subjects, n",
    "Age, years — median (IQR)",
    "Sex — Male, n (%)",
    "Sex — Female, n (%)",
    "Body weight, kg — median (IQR)",
    "BMI, kg/m² — median (IQR)",
    "eGFR, mL/min/1.73m² — median (IQR)"
  ),
  `All subjects (N=12)` = c(
    "12",
    fmt_med_iqr(demo$Age_years),
    fmt_n_pct(demo$Sex,"Male"),
    fmt_n_pct(demo$Sex,"Female"),
    fmt_med_iqr(demo$Weight_kg),
    fmt_med_iqr(demo$BMI),
    fmt_med_iqr(demo$eGFR_ml_min)
  )
)

kable(t1, align=c("l","c")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width=FALSE, position="left") %>%
  row_spec(0, bold=TRUE) %>%
  footnote(general="Self-controlled crossover design. Continuous: median (IQR). eGFR: estimated glomerular filtration rate.",
           general_title="Note: ", footnote_as_chunk=TRUE)
```

### Plasma Concentration–Time Profiles

```{r figure2}
#| label: fig-conc-time
#| fig-cap: "Figure 2. Mean (±SD) midazolam plasma concentration–time profiles with and without CYP3A4 inducer. Orange: without inducer (control). Green: with inducer. Inset: log-scale y-axis showing steeper elimination slope with inducer, confirming shorter t½."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5.5

fig2_main <- ggplot(mean_conc,
    aes(x=TIME, y=mean_conc, colour=ARM, shape=ARM, group=ARM)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin=mean_conc-sd_conc, ymax=mean_conc+sd_conc),
                width=0.2, linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.7) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.85) +
  geom_point(size=2.8, fill="white", stroke=0.9) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values=c("No_inducer"=col_no_ind,"With_inducer"=col_with_ind),
    labels=c("No_inducer"="No inducer","With_inducer"="Inducer"),
    name=NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values=c("No_inducer"=24,"With_inducer"=21),
    labels=c("No_inducer"="No inducer","With_inducer"="Inducer"),
    name=NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks=c(0,2,4,6,8,10,12), limits=c(0,12)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(0,1.65), breaks=seq(0,1.5,by=0.5)) +
  labs(x="Time after dose (h)", y="Concentration (ng/mL)",
       title="Mean midazolam plasma concentration") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position=c(0.85,0.72),
        legend.background=element_rect(fill="white",colour="grey80",linewidth=0.3),
        legend.text=element_text(size=10),
        plot.title=element_text(size=12,face="bold"),
        axis.title=element_text(size=11),
        panel.grid.minor=element_blank())

fig2_inset <- ggplot(mean_conc %>% filter(TIME>0, mean_conc>0),
    aes(x=TIME, y=mean_conc, colour=ARM, group=ARM)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(shape=ARM), size=1.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels=label_log(digits=2), breaks=c(0.03,0.1,0.3,1.0)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=c("No_inducer"=col_no_ind,"With_inducer"=col_with_ind),guide="none") +
  scale_shape_manual(values=c("No_inducer"=24,"With_inducer"=21),guide="none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks=c(0,4,8,12), limits=c(0,12)) +
  labs(x="Time (h)", y="Conc (ng/mL)") +
  theme_bw(base_size=8) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        plot.background=element_rect(fill="white",colour="grey70",linewidth=0.3))

fig2_main + inset_element(fig2_inset, left=0.42, bottom=0.34, right=0.99, top=0.99)
```

Midazolam concentrations were consistently higher in the control arm across all timepoints. Both arms peaked at approximately t = 2 h (T~max~), indicating the inducer did not affect absorption but specifically accelerated elimination. The log-scale inset reveals a steeper descending slope in the inducer arm, directly visualising the accelerated elimination rate constant (λ~z~).

### Noncompartmental Analysis — Table 2

```{r table2}
#| label: tbl-nca
#| tbl-cap: "Table 2. Noncompartmental pharmacokinetic analysis of midazolam in 12 healthy volunteers"

no_ind   <- nca_clean %>% filter(ARM=="No_inducer")   %>% arrange(ID)
with_ind <- nca_clean %>% filter(ARM=="With_inducer") %>% arrange(ID)

fmt <- function(x) {
  x <- na.omit(x)
  paste0(round(median(x),2),
         " (", round(quantile(x,.25),2),
         "–",  round(quantile(x,.75),2),")")
}

get_gmr <- function(param) gmr %>% filter(Parameter==param) %>% pull(GMR_CI)

t2 <- tibble(
  Parameter = c("AUC~last~, ng·h/mL","C~max~, ng/mL",
                "t~½~, h","CL/F, L/h","T~max~, h"),
  `Without inducer (median IQR)` = c(
    fmt(no_ind$AUClast), fmt(no_ind$Cmax),
    fmt(no_ind$t_half),  fmt(no_ind$CLF),
    paste0(median(no_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),
           " (",min(no_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),"–",max(no_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),")")
  ),
  `With inducer (median IQR)` = c(
    fmt(with_ind$AUClast), fmt(with_ind$Cmax),
    fmt(with_ind$t_half),  fmt(with_ind$CLF),
    paste0(median(with_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),
           " (",min(with_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),"–",max(with_ind$Tmax,na.rm=TRUE),")ᵃ")
  ),
  `GMR (95% CI)` = c(
    get_gmr("AUClast"), get_gmr("Cmax"),
    get_gmr("t_half"),  get_gmr("CL_F"), "NA"
  )
)

kable(t2, align=c("l","c","c","c"), escape=FALSE) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options=c("striped","hover","condensed"), full_width=TRUE) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold=TRUE) %>%
  row_spec(1, bold=TRUE) %>%
  footnote(
    alphabet="Wilcoxon signed-rank test, p < 0.05.",
    general=paste0("GMR = geometric mean ratio (with / without inducer). ",
                   "CI = 95% confidence interval (paired t-test, log-transformed). ",
                   "Bioequivalence no-effect boundaries: 0.80–1.25."),
    general_title="", footnote_as_chunk=TRUE)
```

### Individual Pharmacokinetic Variability

```{r figure3}
#| label: fig-spaghetti
#| fig-cap: "Figure 3. Individual midazolam apparent clearance (CL/F) with and without CYP3A4 inducer. Each grey line represents one subject (n=12). All subjects showed increased CL/F with the inducer, confirming universal CYP3A4 induction."
#| fig-width: 5.5
#| fig-height: 5.5

clf_long <- nca_clean %>%
  select(ID, ARM, CLF) %>%
  mutate(ARM_label = ifelse(ARM=="No_inducer","No inducer","Inducer"),
         ARM_label = factor(ARM_label, levels=c("No inducer","Inducer")))

ggplot(clf_long, aes(x=ARM_label, y=CLF, group=ID)) +
  geom_line(colour="grey55", linewidth=0.65, alpha=0.75) +
  geom_point(aes(colour=ARM), size=3.2, alpha=0.9) +
  scale_colour_manual(values=c("No_inducer"=col_no_ind,"With_inducer"=col_with_ind),
                      guide="none") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(1500,6800), breaks=seq(2000,6000,1000),
                     labels=comma) +
  labs(x=NULL,
       y=expression("Apparent clearance CL/F (L h"^{-1}~")"),
       title="Individual midazolam CL/F",
       caption="Each line = one subject. Orange = no inducer. Green = with inducer.") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text=element_text(size=12),
        axis.title.y=element_text(size=11),
        plot.title=element_text(size=12, face="bold"),
        panel.grid.minor=element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x=element_blank())
```

All 12 subjects showed increased CL/F with the inducer — confirming universal CYP3A4 induction with no non-responders. Inter-individual variability in the magnitude of induction reflects differences in baseline CYP3A4 expression and inducibility.

### Primary Endpoint: AUC~last~ GMR

```{r gmr-result}
auc <- gmr %>% filter(Parameter=="AUClast")
pct_red <- round((1 - auc$GMR) * 100, 0)
```

**GMR for AUC~last~ = `r auc$GMR` (95% CI: `r auc$CI_lower`–`r auc$CI_upper`)**

The inducer caused a **`r pct_red`% reduction** in midazolam systemic exposure. The entire 95% CI lies below the 0.80 bioequivalence boundary — confirming a **clinically significant DDI**.

Supporting parameters:

- **C~max~ GMR = `r gmr %>% filter(Parameter=="Cmax") %>% pull(GMR)`** — peak concentration reduced, indicating gut-wall CYP3A contribution
- **t~½~ GMR = `r gmr %>% filter(Parameter=="t_half") %>% pull(GMR)`** — elimination half-life shortened, confirming accelerated hepatic metabolism
- **CL/F GMR = `r gmr %>% filter(Parameter=="CL_F") %>% pull(GMR)`** — apparent clearance increased, reciprocal of AUC reduction

---

## Discussion

The CYP3A4 inducer reduced midazolam AUC~last~ by `r pct_red`%, increased CL/F, and shortened t~½~. All findings confirm CYP3A4-mediated enzyme induction. The GMR of `r auc$GMR` with the entire 95% CI below 0.80 classifies this as a clinically significant DDI per EMA/FDA guidance.

This reproduction confirms that open-source R tools (`PKNCA`, `ggplot2`, `tableone`) can produce publication-quality clinical PK analyses matching published results.

### Limitations

This analysis used a simulated dataset. Real studies additionally require CYP3A5 genotyping, 1-OH-midazolam metabolite measurement, and validated bioanalytical methods.

---

## Conclusion

A CYP3A4 inducer significantly reduced midazolam systemic exposure (AUC~last~ GMR = `r auc$GMR`; 95% CI `r auc$CI_lower`–`r auc$CI_upper`), confirming a clinically significant DDI with `r pct_red`% reduction in exposure. Results reproduce Dunvald et al. (2022) Table 2 closely using PKNCA with linear-up/log-down trapezoidal NCA.

---

## Reproducibility

```{r session-info}
#| code-fold: false
sessionInfo()
```
